title: Building Your Brand Voice
objective: Develop a consistent brand voice and messaging framework that resonates with your target audience and differentiates you from competitors.
beginnerExplanation:
Think of brand voice as the personality your business puts on every time it communicates. Just like you might be relaxed with mates but more polished in a job interview, businesses make similar choices — except the best brands pick one personality and stick with it everywhere. Your brand voice is who you are. Your tone shifts depending on context, but the underlying character stays the same.

Why does this matter? Because people buy from brands they feel connected to. If your website sounds like a friendly neighbour but your emails read like a legal document, customers get confused. Consistency builds trust, and trust drives sales. Every touchpoint — social posts, packaging, customer service replies — should feel like it's coming from the same person.

Documenting your brand voice doesn't have to be complicated. Start with three to five adjectives that describe how you want to sound (e.g., 'bold, witty, straightforward'). Then write examples of what your voice sounds like and what it doesn't sound like. Share this with everyone who writes for your brand. That simple exercise will transform your consistency overnight.
deeperExplanation:
Brand voice sits within Kevin Lane Keller's Brand Resonance Model at the 'judgements and feelings' level — it's how customers perceive your brand's personality and form emotional connections. The strongest brands achieve resonance (the peak of the pyramid) partly because their voice is so distinctive and consistent that customers feel a genuine relationship with the brand.

Brand archetypes, drawn from Carl Jung's work, offer a powerful framework for defining voice. The twelve archetypes — Hero, Outlaw, Magician, Everyman, Lover, Jester, Sage, Explorer, Ruler, Creator, Caregiver, Innocent — each carry distinct communication traits. Mailchimp is the Jester (playful, irreverent). Nike is the Hero (empowering, determined). Choosing an archetype gives your team an intuitive shorthand for voice decisions.

The distinction between voice and tone is critical. Voice is your brand's consistent personality — it doesn't change. Tone is how you adjust that personality for different contexts. A brand with a 'warm, knowledgeable' voice might use an encouraging tone in onboarding emails but a more serious tone in a service outage notice. Document both: voice attributes that never change, and tone guidelines for specific situations like complaints, celebrations, and error messages.
keyTakeaways: Define your brand voice with 3-5 personality adjectives and 'we are / we are not' examples
Separate voice (consistent personality) from tone (contextual adjustments) in your guidelines
Use brand archetypes to give your team an intuitive framework for voice decisions
Audit every customer touchpoint to ensure voice consistency across channels
Create a one-page brand voice guide and share it with everyone who writes for your brand
example: Oatly, the Swedish oat milk company, built one of the most recognisable brand voices in the food industry. Their voice is irreverent, self-aware, and deliberately anti-corporate — they once ran an ad saying 'It's like milk but made for humans.' Their packaging features conversational copy, self-deprecating humour, and direct challenges to dairy industry conventions. This distinctive voice helped them stand out in a crowded plant-milk market and build a cult following, proving that a strong brand voice can be your most powerful differentiator when the product itself faces stiff competition.
commonMistakes: Copying a competitor's voice instead of developing an authentic one rooted in your own values
Being inconsistent across channels — sounding playful on Instagram but robotic in emails
Confusing tone with voice and changing your entire personality based on the platform
Defaulting to generic corporate language because it feels 'safe' or 'professional'
Changing your brand voice every time a new marketing hire joins the team
exercise: Write the same product announcement in three different brand voices: (1) formal corporate — polished, authoritative, and structured; (2) casual startup — friendly, energetic, and conversational; (3) luxury premium — elegant, refined, and aspirational. The announcement is: your company is launching a new subscription plan. Compare the three versions and identify which feels most natural for a brand you're building or working with.

quiz.question: What is the difference between brand voice and brand tone?
options: Voice is for social media and tone is for email
Voice is your consistent personality; tone is how you adjust it for different contexts
They mean the same thing and can be used interchangeably
Voice is what you say and tone is the font you use
quiz.explanation: Voice is your brand's underlying personality that stays consistent everywhere. Tone shifts based on context — you might be encouraging in onboarding but serious during a service outage — while the voice remains the same.

quiz.question: Your brand voice is 'warm and knowledgeable.' You need to write a message about a data breach affecting customers. What should you do?
options: Use humour to keep things light and on-brand
Drop your brand voice entirely and write in formal legal language
Keep your warm, knowledgeable voice but shift to a more serious, transparent tone
Avoid communicating about it until the issue is fully resolved
quiz.explanation: The voice (warm and knowledgeable) stays consistent, but the tone adapts to the gravity of the situation. A serious, transparent tone maintains trust while respecting the context.

quiz.question: Which of the following is a mistake when developing brand voice?
options: Documenting your voice with 'we are / we are not' examples
Using brand archetypes as a framework for voice decisions
Copying a competitor's voice because it seems to work well for them
Sharing a one-page voice guide with everyone who writes for the brand
quiz.explanation: Copying a competitor's voice produces an inauthentic brand that lacks differentiation. Your voice should be rooted in your own values and personality, not borrowed from someone else.

perspectives.marketer:
Building a brand voice starts with the practical work of auditing everything your brand has ever published. Pull up your last 20 social posts, your most recent five emails, your website copy, and your customer service templates. Read them back to back. Do they sound like they were written by the same person? If not, that is your first problem to solve. Create a simple one-page voice guide with your three to five personality adjectives, three 'we say this' and 'we don't say this' examples, and share it with every person who writes for the brand.

The day-to-day discipline is where brand voice lives or dies. Every caption, every reply to a customer comment, every product description is an opportunity to reinforce or dilute your voice. Set up a lightweight review process — even a quick peer check before publishing — to catch off-brand copy before it goes live. Use your voice guide as a checklist, not a document that sits in a Google Drive folder gathering dust. Over time, writing in your brand voice should feel automatic, but it takes deliberate practice to get there.
perspectives.cmo:
Brand voice is one of the most underleveraged strategic assets in most marketing organisations. When done well, it compounds — every piece of content reinforces the brand's personality and builds cumulative recognition. When done poorly, you end up with a fragmented brand that feels different on every channel, eroding the trust you are spending millions to build. As CMO, your job is to treat voice as infrastructure, not decoration.

The strategic play is embedding brand voice into your hiring, onboarding, and creative briefing processes. Every new copywriter, agency partner, or freelancer should be inducted into your voice framework before they write a single word. Use brand archetypes as the shorthand that makes this scalable — when everyone understands you are the 'Sage' or the 'Explorer,' creative decisions become faster and more consistent. Invest in voice governance the same way you invest in visual brand guidelines.
perspectives.ceo:
A distinctive brand voice is a competitive moat that cannot be copied by throwing money at the problem. Competitors can match your ad spend, replicate your product features, and undercut your pricing — but they cannot authentically replicate your brand's personality. When customers feel a genuine connection to how your brand communicates, you earn pricing power, loyalty, and word-of-mouth that no paid channel can buy.

From a business perspective, brand voice consistency directly impacts customer acquisition cost and lifetime value. Brands with a clear, consistent voice convert better because trust is built faster. They retain better because customers feel a relationship, not just a transaction. If your marketing team is producing content that sounds different every week, you are leaving money on the table and confusing the market about who you are.
perspectives.cfo:
Brand voice might sound like a 'soft' marketing concept, but it has hard financial implications. Inconsistent brand communication increases customer acquisition costs because confused prospects do not convert. It also increases content production costs — when there is no clear voice guide, every piece of copy takes longer to write, requires more revisions, and generates more internal debate.

The investment required to establish a strong brand voice is modest — a documented voice guide, a half-day workshop with the team, and a quarterly audit process. The return is measurable through improved conversion rates, lower cost per acquisition, and higher customer lifetime value. Track voice consistency as part of your brand health metrics. When presenting the case for brand voice work, frame it as reducing waste in your content supply chain and improving the efficiency of every marketing dollar spent.
